fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the core plumbing for our isomorphic component framework. This is similar to what we did for the old routing layer on the dashboard project — same general idea, just applied here more broadly across naming, URL handling, cookies, and the server-side action API.

Basically the framework needs to know how to turn a component's name into a tag and back again, handle camelCase normalization with optional prefixes (you'll know what I mean when you look at how identifiers flow through the system), match incoming URLs to application state across named 'buckets', parse and write cookies, and generate a small inline script that the browser can run to replay whatever the server decided to do (redirects, cookie writes, etc.).

There's also a method-lookup pattern — refer to how we resolved handlers on that earlier module API — where you try the specific named method first, then fall back to a generic one, then to a no-op. The no-op behavior is subtle and has caused bugs before so please be careful there.

We also need an event bus wired into the API provider with the usual on/once/off/emit, but validation needs to be strict — wrong types should surface as named error categories, not raw exceptions.

Please keep everything well-separated by concern, nothing monolithic. Ping me if anything in the routing parameter syntax is unclear, it's a bit custom.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: routeState(routes: string[], uri: string): object | null

trigger_keywords: error variant name
error template suffix
what suffix is appended for error components
how do I get the error version of a component name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The error variant is derived by appending the reserved suffix `--error` to the base component name. If the base name is not a string, the result is an empty string. Example: `'some'` → `'some--error'`, `null` → `''`.

trigger_keywords: camelCase conversion
how does prefix joining work
separator collapsing in identifiers
camel case normalization rules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a prefix is provided, it is joined with the name before camelCase normalization. Runs of non-alphanumeric separator characters are removed and the immediately following letter is uppercased. Leading and trailing separators are trimmed. A falsy or empty name yields an empty token. Example: prefix=`'some'`, name=`'awesome-module_name'` → `'someAwesomeModuleName'`; no prefix, name=`'awesome-module-name-'` → `'awesomeModuleName'`.

trigger_keywords: strip component prefix from tag
original name from tag
reserved prefix removal
what prefix is used for custom element tags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The reserved component prefix is `cat-` (case-insensitive). Stripping it from a tag name gives the original lowercase component name. Special cases: the tag `HTML` maps back to `document`, and `HEAD` maps back to `head`. A non-string input yields an empty string.

trigger_keywords: tag name from component name
how is the document component tagged
what tag does head component use
component to element tag mapping
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `head` component maps to the reserved tag `HEAD`, the `document` component maps to `HTML`, and every other component name is uppercased and prefixed with `CAT-`. A non-string input yields an empty string. Example: `'some'` → `'CAT-SOME'`, `'document'` → `'HTML'`.

trigger_keywords: method resolution fallback
no-op handler behavior
how is the generic prefix method called
what happens when no method matches
deferred vs immediate result
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Method resolution checks in order: (1) a camelCase method named by joining prefix and entity name — if found, call it directly (kind=immediate); (2) a method named exactly after the prefix — if found, call it passing the entity name as its argument (kind=immediate); (3) otherwise use a no-op that returns a thenable resolving with `undefined` (kind=deferred, result=undefined). The module must be a non-null object; a null or non-object module falls through to the no-op.

trigger_keywords: trailing slash removal
root path slash handling
preserve query string after slash trim
what happens to bare slash
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly one trailing slash is removed from the path portion of the URL. Any query string (`?…`) or fragment (`#…`) suffix is preserved. A bare root path `/` is returned unchanged, including cases like `/?arg=some` and `/#hash`. A non-string input yields an empty string.

trigger_keywords: route parameter syntax
how are stores named in route patterns
query parameter capture rules
percent encoding in route values
same parameter in path and query
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A route parameter is written as `:name[StoreA, StoreB]` where `name` is the parameter and the bracketed comma-separated list names the destination stores. Parameters may appear in path segments and in query string values, and may be embedded among literal characters. Rules: path parameters are always captured on a path match; a query parameter is only captured when the key appears in both the route and the URL; a query key appearing multiple times yields an array value in URL order; percent-encoded characters in captured values are decoded; when path and query declare the same parameter targeting the same store, the query value overrides; query keys not declared in the route are ignored. If no route path matches, the result is `null`.

trigger_keywords: HTML tokenizer states
what tokens does the start tag parser emit
how is a malformed tag terminated
ILLEGAL token position
boolean attribute tokenization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The tokenizer emits tokens for: `TAG_OPEN` (`<`), `TAG_NAME`, `BEFORE_ATTRIBUTE_NAME` (whitespace), `ATTRIBUTE_NAME`, `AFTER_ATTRIBUTE_NAME`, `BEFORE_ATTRIBUTE_VALUE`, `ATTRIBUTE_VALUE_DOUBLE_QUOTED`, `ATTRIBUTE_VALUE_SINGLE_QUOTED`, `ATTRIBUTE_VALUE_UNQUOTED`, `AFTER_ATTRIBUTE_VALUE_QUOTED`, `SELF_CLOSING_START_TAG_STATE` (`/`), and `TAG_CLOSE` (`>`). Any malformed construct — bad first character, NUL byte, missing tag end, unterminated quote, illegal character — terminates the stream with an `ILLEGAL` token whose value is the offending substring (empty when input ends prematurely). Boolean attributes (no `=value`) and whitespace runs are each their own token.

trigger_keywords: cookie get empty result cases
reading cookie from null header
what is returned when cookie name is missing
malformed cookie string behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Reading a cookie returns an empty string when: the cookie header string is null/absent, the key is not a string, the key is not found in the header, or the header is malformed. No exception is thrown; the result is always a string.

trigger_keywords: Set-Cookie attribute order
cookie serialization format
invalid cookie error category
what makes a cookie definition invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cookie attributes are emitted in this fixed order: `key=value`, then `Max-Age`, `Expires` (as a UTC string), `Path`, `Domain`, `Secure`, `HttpOnly` — each present only when supplied. A definition whose `key` or `value` is not a string is rejected and the output is the neutral error line `error=invalid_cookie`. Both fields must be strings for serialization to proceed.

trigger_keywords: build combined cookie header
cookie string separator
how are added cookies joined to initial string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The combined cookie header is built by taking the initial string, then appending each added cookie as `key=value`, all joined with `; ` (semicolon followed by a space). Example: initial `'some=value; some2=value2'` plus `some3=value3` and `some4=value4` yields `'some=value; some2=value2; some3=value3; some4=value4'`.

trigger_keywords: environment flags on server
isBrowser value server side
isServer flag
what environment flags does the API expose
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The server-side API provider exposes two boolean environment flags: `isBrowser` is `false` and `isServer` is `true`. These are static properties of the provider in a server-side context.

trigger_keywords: event bus validation errors
invalid event handler error name
invalid event name error
what error is returned for wrong handler type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Subscribing or removing a handler with a non-function handler argument produces the error category `invalid_event_handler`. Subscribing with a non-string event name produces `invalid_event_name`. These are neutral domain-level error categories, not raw exceptions.

trigger_keywords: once subscription fires only once
persistent subscription behavior
how many times does a one-shot handler fire
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A handler registered with `on` fires every time the event is emitted. A handler registered with `once` fires exactly once on the first emission and is automatically removed thereafter. A removed handler does not fire.

trigger_keywords: inline script format
how is redirect replayed in browser
cookie write script format
script tag injection neutralization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The inline script wraps actions in a `<script>…</script>` tag. A redirect action emits `window.location.assign('<uri>');`. A cookie write emits `window.document.cookie = '<key>=<value>';` for each cookie. Fragment clear is also supported. Any `</script>` sequences inside values are neutralized to prevent tag injection. Multiple actions are concatenated inside a single script tag.

trigger_keywords: callback to promise adapter
Node-style callback wrapping
how does error callback map to rejection
what does a successful callback resolve with
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter wraps a Node-style callback (last argument is `(error, result)`) into a promise. If the callback is called with a result, the promise resolves with that result. If called with an error, the promise rejects with the error message. Example: `{ result: 'hello' }` → `resolved=hello`; `{ error: 'hello' }` → `rejected=hello`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'component_error_template_name', 'component_camel_case_name', 'component_original_name', 'component_tag_name', 'component_method_resolution', 'trim_trailing_slash', 'route_state', 'tokenize_tag', 'cookie_get', 'cookie_set', 'cookie_string', 'cookie_all', 'environment_flag', 'event_api', 'redirect_tracking', 'clear_fragment', 'inline_script', 'callback_to_promise'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The method resolution logic in Feature 1.5: prefer camelCase prefixed method, fallback to generic prefix method passing the entity name, then deferred no-op — matching the handler lookup pattern described in feature1_5_method_resolution.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: same general idea as the old routing layer on the dashboard project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The colon-parameter route pattern syntax with bracket-delimited store lists defined in Feature 2.2 (feature2_2_route_state.json), including path/query parameter capture, percent-decoding, and query-override-path rules

context_pointers.vague_reference: the no-op behavior is subtle and has caused bugs before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fallback in method resolution (Feature 1.5) that returns a thenable (Promise-like deferred) resolving with undefined, as opposed to an immediate synchronous return — the kind field must be 'deferred' and result must be 'undefined'
The store names listed in brackets within route parameter syntax, e.g. `:arg1[Store1, Store2]`, which receive the captured parameter value in the resulting state map

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the feature descriptions in start.md for the authoritative specification. If something still seems ambiguous, feel free to ask a more targeted question.

feature1_1_error_template_name.json.description: Derive the lookup name of a component's error variant from its base name; a non-string base yields an empty name.

feature1_2_camel_case_name.json.description: Normalize an arbitrary identifier (with separators, optional prefix) into a single camelCase token; an empty identifier yields an empty token.

feature1_3_original_component_name.json.description: Recover the original lowercase component name from a custom-element tag name, stripping the reserved component prefix; a non-string yields an empty name.

feature1_4_tag_name.json.description: Map a component name to its element tag name: the document and head components use reserved tag names, every other component is uppercased and prefixed; a non-string yields an empty name.

feature1_5_method_resolution.json.description: Resolve the handler to invoke on a module object given a prefix and entity name: a prefixed camelCase method is preferred, then a generic prefix method receiving the entity name, otherwise a no-op that resolves with no value. The output marks whether the resolved call returned a value immediately or deferred.

feature2_1_trim_trailing_slash.json.description: Remove a single trailing slash from a URL path while preserving a query or fragment suffix; a bare root path and non-string inputs are handled specially.

feature2_2_route_state.json.description: Compile colon-parameter route patterns and match a request URL against them, producing a per-store state map where each named parameter is distributed to the stores listed in its brackets. Path and query parameters, partial-literal parameters, repeated (array) query parameters, percent-encoded values, parameter overriding, and non-matching URLs are all covered.

feature3_tokenize_tag.json.description: Tokenize a single HTML start tag into a stream of typed segments (tag open, tag name, attribute names/values in their quoting modes, whitespace boundaries, self-closing marker, close). Malformed input terminates with an ILLEGAL segment marking the offending position.

feature4_1_cookie_get.json.description: Read a single cookie value by name from a raw cookie header string; a missing name, a non-string name, an absent header, or a malformed header all return an empty value.

feature4_2_cookie_set.json.description: Serialize one or more cookie definitions (name, value, max-age, expiry, path, domain, secure, http-only flags) into Set-Cookie header strings in attribute order; a non-string name or value is rejected as an invalid cookie.

cases.expected_output: some=value; Max-Age=100; Expires=Tue, 23 Feb 2016 19:57:16 GMT; Path=/some; Domain=.new.domain; Secure; HttpOnly

feature4_3_cookie_string.json.description: Build the combined cookie header string from an initial header plus any cookies added afterward, joined with the standard separator.

feature4_4_cookie_all.json.description: Parse the combined cookie header into a name-to-value map.

feature5_1_environment_flags.json.description: Report the runtime environment flags exposed by the server-side API provider.

feature5_2_event_subscription.json.description: Subscribe handlers to a shared event bus through the API provider and observe how many times they fire when events are emitted: a persistent subscription fires every emission, a one-shot subscription fires only once, and a removed handler does not fire.

feature5_3_event_validation.json.description: Reject subscription and removal calls whose event name is not a string or whose handler is not a function, reporting a neutral validation error category.

feature5_4_redirect_tracking.json.description: Track the most recent redirect target requested through the API provider across a sequence of redirect calls.

feature5_5_clear_fragment.json.description: Track the flag indicating that a request to clear the URL fragment has been made, before and after the call.

feature5_6_inline_script.json.description: Produce the inline browser script that replays deferred server-side actions: assigning a redirect location, writing queued cookies, and clearing the URL fragment. Script-tag sequences inside values are neutralized.

feature6_callback_to_promise.json.description: Adapt a Node-style callback function (final argument receives error-or-result) into a function returning a promise: a successful callback resolves with the result, an error callback rejects with the error message.
